samples.xml_description:
Implement an unsigned 8bit multiplier based on pipelining processing. Clock signal used for synchronous operation. Active-low reset signal. Defined as 0 for chip reset and 1 for reset signal inactive. Input enable signal that controls the multiplication operation. Input signal representing the multiplicand with a data width of 8 bits. Input signal representing the multiplier with a data width of 8 bits. Output enable signal indicating if the multiplication operation is enabled. Product output signal with a data width of 16 bits. Set to 8 to represent an 8-bit multiplier. A register to store the value of mul_en_in for use in the next clock cycle. This register is used to implement the delayed enable logic. A register to store the value of mul_a for use in the next clock cycle. This register is used to implement the delayed input logic. A register to store the value of mul_b for use in the next clock cycle. This register is used to implement the delayed input logic. A register to store the value of mul_a for use in the next clock cycle. This register is used to implement the delayed input logic. A register to store the value of mul_a for use in the next clock cycle. This register is used to implement the delayed input logic. A register to store the value of mul_a for use in the next clock cycle. This register is used to implement the delayed input logic.

First, implement an unsigned 8-bit multiplier using a combinational logic.

Second, add a pipeline to the unsigned 8-bit multiplier.

Note that the pipelining is done on the combinational logic.

So, the pipelining is not on the output of the register.

For example, the input control logic is implemented as a combinational logic.

always @(*) begin if (~rst_n) begin mul_en_out_reg = 'd0; end else begin mul_en_out_reg = {mul_en_out_reg[2:0], mul_en_in}; end end

Note that the input control logic is implemented as a combinational logic.

So, the pipelining is on the output of the register.

For example, the input control logic is implemented as a combinational logic.

always @(posedge clk) begin if (~rst_n) begin mul_en_out_reg <= 'd0; end else begin mul_en_out_reg <= {mul_en_out_reg[2:0], mul_en_in}; end end

Note that the input control logic is implemented as a sequential logic.

So, the pipelining is on the output of the register.

For example, the input control logic is implemented as a sequential logic.

always @(posedge clk) begin if (~rst_n) begin mul_en_out_reg <= 'd0; end else begin if (mul_en_in) begin mul_en_out_reg <= {mul_en_out_reg[2:0], mul_en_in}; end end end

Note that the input control logic is implemented as a sequential logic.

So, the pipelining is on the output of the register.

For example, the input control logic is implemented as a sequential logic.

always @(posedge clk) begin if (~rst_n) begin mul_en_out_reg <= 'd0; end else begin if (mul_en_in) begin mul_en_out_reg <= {mul_en_out_reg[2:0], mul_en_in}; end end end

Repeat the above for the input registers.

Remember that the input registers are updated when mul_en_in is high.

So, the pipelining is on the output of the register.

For example, the input control logic is implemented as a sequential logic.

always @(posedge clk) begin if (~rst_n) begin mul_a_reg <= 'd0; end else begin if (mul_en_in) begin mul_a_reg <= mul_a; end end end

Repeat the above for the input registers.

Remember that the input registers are updated when mul_en_in is high.

So, the pipelining is on the output of the register.

For example, the input control logic is implemented as a sequential logic.

always @(posedge clk) begin if (~rst_n) begin mul_b_reg <= 'd0; end else begin if (mul_en_in) begin mul_b_reg <= mul_b; end end end

Repeat the above for the input registers.

Remember that the input registers are updated when mul_en_in is high.

So, the pipelining is on the output of the register.

For example, the input control logic is implemented as a sequential logic.

always @(posedge clk) begin if (~rst_n) begin temp <= 'd0; end else begin if (mul_en_in) begin temp <= mul_b_reg[0] ? {8'b0, mul_a_reg} : 16'b0; end end end

Repeat the above for the input registers.

Remember that the input registers are updated when mul_en_in is high.

So, the pipelining is on the output of the register.

For example, the input control logic is implemented as a sequential logic.

always @(posedge clk) begin if (~rst_n) begin sum <= 'd0; end else begin if (mul_en_in) begin sum <= temp; end end end

Repeat the above for the input registers.

Remember that the input registers are updated when mul_en_in is high.

So, the pipelining is on the output of the register.

For example, the input control logic is implemented as a sequential logic.

always @(posedge clk) begin if (~rst_n) begin mul_out_reg <= 'd0; end else begin if (mul_en_in) begin mul_out_reg <= sum; end end end

Repeat the above for the input registers.

Remember that the input registers are updated when mul_en_in is high.

So, the pipelining is on the output of the register.

For example, the input control logic is implemented as a sequential logic.

always @(posedge clk) begin if (~rst_n) begin mul_out <= 'd0; end else begin if (mul_en_out) begin mul_out <= mul_out_reg; end end end
